text.Rotherham: Then said Moses unto Aaron and to Eleazar and to Ithamar, his sons—Your heads, ye may not bare and your garments, ye shall not rend so shall ye not die, neither against all the assembly, will he be wroth,—but let, your brethren the whole house of Israel, bewail the consuming fire wherewith Yahweh hath consumed.

text.NRSV: You shall not go outside the entrance of the tent of meeting, or you will die; for the anointing oil of the Lord is on you.” And they did as Moses had ordered.
text.Rotherham: But, from the entrance of the tent of meeting, shall ye not go forth, lest ye die, for, the anointing oil of Yahweh is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses.

text.NRSV: And the Lord spoke to Aaron:
text.Rotherham: Then spake Yahweh unto Aaron, saying:

text.NRSV: Drink no wine or strong drink, neither you nor your sons, when you enter the tent of meeting, that you may not die; it is a statute forever throughout your generations.
text.Rotherham: Wine and strong drink, thou mayest not drink,—thou nor thy sons with thee when ye enter into the tent of meeting, so shall ye not die,—an age-abiding statute to your generations;

text.NRSV: You are to distinguish between the holy and the common, and between the unclean and the clean;
text.Rotherham: That ye may make a difference, between the sacred and the common,—and between the unclean and the clean;

text.NRSV: and you are to teach the people of Israel all the statutes that the Lord has spoken to them through Moses.
text.Rotherham: And may teach the sons of Israel,—all the statutes which Yahweh hath spoken unto them, by the mediation of Moses.

text.NRSV: Moses spoke to Aaron and to his remaining sons, Eleazar and Ithamar: “Take the grain offering that is left from the Lord’s offerings by fire, and eat it unleavened beside the altar, for it is most holy;
text.Rotherham: Then spake Moses unto Aaron and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar his sons that were left, Take ye the meal-offering that is left of the altar-flames of Yahweh, and eat it unleavened beside the altar,—for, most holy, it is;

text.NRSV: you shall eat it in a holy place, because it is your due and your sons’ due, from the offerings by fire to the Lord; for so I am commanded.
text.Rotherham: therefore shall ye eat it in a holy place, for, thine by statute, and thy sons’ by statute, it is, from among the altar-flames of Yahweh,—for, so, am I commanded.

text.NRSV: But the breast that is elevated and the thigh that is raised, you and your sons and daughters as well may eat in any clean place; for they have been assigned to you and your children from the sacrifices of the offerings of well-being of the people of Israel.
text.Rotherham: And the wave-breast, and the heave-shoulder, shall ye eat in clean place, thou, and thy sons and thy daughters with thee,—for as thine by statute and thy sons’ by statute, have they been given, from among the peace-offerings of the sons of Israel.

text.NRSV: The thigh that is raised and the breast that is elevated they shall bring, together with the offerings by fire of the fat, to raise for an elevation offering before the Lord; they are to be your due and that of your children forever, as the Lord has commanded.
text.Rotherham: The heave-shoulder and the wave-breast upon the altar-flames of the fat portions, shall they bring in, to wave as a wave-offering, before Yahweh,—so shall they be thine, and thy sons’ with thee, by an age-abiding statute, As Yahweh hath commanded.

text.NRSV: Then Moses made inquiry about the goat of the sin offering, and—it had already been burned! He was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, Aaron’s remaining sons, and said,
text.Rotherham: Now, as for the sin-bearing goat, Moses diligently sought it but lo! it had been burnt up,—then was he wroth against Eleazar and against Ithamar, the sons of Aaron that were left, saying:

text.NRSV: “Why did you not eat the sin offering in the sacred area? For it is most holy, and God has given it to you that you may remove the guilt of the congregation, to make atonement on their behalf before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore did ye not eat the sin-bearer in the holy place? for most, holy, it is,—and thee same, hath he given you that ye may bear the iniquity of the assembly, to put a propitiatory-covering over them before Yahweh.

text.NRSV: Its blood was not brought into the inner part of the sanctuary. You should certainly have eaten it in the sanctuary, as I commanded.”
text.Rotherham: Lo! the blood thereof had not been taken into the holy place, within,—ye should have, indeed eaten, it, in a holy place, as I commanded.

text.NRSV: And Aaron spoke to Moses, “See, today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the Lord; and yet such things as these have befallen me! If I had eaten the sin offering today, would it have been acceptable to the Lord?”
text.Rotherham: Then spake Aaron unto Moses—Lo! this very day, when they had brought near their own sin-bearer, and their own ascending-sacrifice, there befell me such things as these,—if, then, I had eaten of the sin-bearer this day, would it have been well-pleasing in the eyes of Yahweh?

text.NRSV: And when Moses heard that, he agreed.
text.Rotherham: And, when Moses heard that, then was it well-pleasing in his eyes.

text.NRSV: The Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying unto them:

text.NRSV: Speak to the people of Israel, saying: From among all the land animals these are the creatures that you may eat.
text.Rotherham: Speak ye unto the sons of Israel saying,—These are the living things which ye may eat, of all the beasts which are upon the earth:

text.NRSV: Any animal that has divided hoofs and is cleft-footed and chews the cud—such you may eat.
text.Rotherham: Whatsoever parteth the hoof and is cloven-footed, and cheweth the cud among beasts, that, may ye eat.

text.NRSV: But among those that chew the cud or have divided hoofs, you shall not eat the following: the camel, for even though it chews the cud, it does not have divided hoofs; it is unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: Nevertheless, these, shall ye not eat, of them that chew the cud, and of them that part the hoof,—the camel, because though he, cheweth the cud, yet, the hoof, he parteth not, unclean, he is to you;

text.NRSV: The rock badger, for even though it chews the cud, it does not have divided hoofs; it is unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: And, the coney, because, though he cheweth the cud, yet, the hoof, he parteth not,—unclean, he is to you;

text.NRSV: The hare, for even though it chews the cud, it does not have divided hoofs; it is unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: And the hare, because though she cheweth the cud, yet the hoof, she parteth not,—unclean, she is to you;

text.NRSV: The pig, for even though it has divided hoofs and is cleft-footed, it does not chew the cud; it is unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: And, the swine, because though he parteth the hoof, and is cloven-footed, yet, the cud, he cheweth not,—unclean, he is to you;

text.NRSV: Of their flesh you shall not eat, and their carcasses you shall not touch; they are unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: Of their flesh, shall ye not eat, and, their carcase, shall ye not touch,—unclean, they are to you.

text.NRSV: These you may eat, of all that are in the waters. Everything in the waters that has fins and scales, whether in the seas or in the streams—such you may eat.
text.Rotherham: these, may ye eat, of all that are in the waters,—all that have fins and scales in the waters, in the seas and in the rivers, them, may ye eat.

text.NRSV: But anything in the seas or the streams that does not have fins and scales, of the swarming creatures in the waters and among all the other living creatures that are in the waters—they are detestable to you
text.Rotherham: But, all that have not fins and scales, in the seas and in the rivers, of all that swarm in the waters, and of all the living souls that are in the waters, an abomination, they are unto you;

text.NRSV: and detestable they shall remain. Of their flesh you shall not eat, and their carcasses you shall regard as detestable.
text.Rotherham: and an abomination, shall they remain to you,—of their flesh, ye shall not eat, and their carcases, shall ye abhor.

text.NRSV: Everything in the waters that does not have fins and scales is detestable to you.
text.Rotherham: Whatsoever hath not fins and scales in the waters, an abomination, it is unto you.

text.NRSV: These you shall regard as detestable among the birds. They shall not be eaten; they are an abomination: the eagle, the vulture, the osprey,
text.Rotherham: And, these, shall ye abhor of birds, they shall not be eaten, an abomination, they are,—the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the osprey;

text.NRSV: the buzzard, the kite of any kind;
text.Rotherham: and the vulture, and the falcon, after its kind;

text.NRSV: every raven of any kind;
text.Rotherham: every raven, after its kind;

text.NRSV: the ostrich, the nighthawk, the sea gull, the hawk of any kind;
text.Rotherham: and the female ostrich, and the male ostrich, and the sea-gull,—and the hawk after its kind;

text.NRSV: the little owl, the cormorant, the great owl,
text.Rotherham: and the pelican and the gannet, and the bittern;

text.NRSV: the water hen, the desert owl, the carrion vulture,
text.Rotherham: and the swan and the vomiting pelican and the little vulture;

text.NRSV: the stork, the heron of any kind, the hoopoe, and the bat.
text.Rotherham: and the stork, and the parrot, after its kind,—and the mountain-cock and the bat.

text.NRSV: All winged insects that walk upon all fours are detestable to you.
text.Rotherham: Every creeping thing that flieth, that goeth on all-fours, an abomination, it is unto you.

text.NRSV: But among the winged insects that walk on all fours you may eat those that have jointed legs above their feet, with which to leap on the ground.
text.Rotherham: Nevertheless, these, may ye eat, of all creeping things that fly, that go on all-fours,—such as have legs above their feet, to leap therewith, upon the earth—

text.NRSV: Of them you may eat: the locust according to its kind, the bald locust according to its kind, the cricket according to its kind, and the grasshopper according to its kind.
text.Rotherham: these of them, may ye eat: the swarming-locust after its kind, and the devouring locust after its kind,—and the chargol-locust after its kind, and the chagab-locust after its kind.

text.NRSV: But all other winged insects that have four feet are detestable to you.
text.Rotherham: But every [other] creeping thing that flieth, which hath four feet, an abomination, it is unto you;

text.NRSV: By these you shall become unclean; whoever touches their carcass shall be unclean until the evening,
text.Rotherham: and, for these, shall ye count yourselves unclean—whosoever toucheth the carcase of them shall be unclean until the evening;

text.NRSV: and whoever carries any part of their carcass shall wash his clothes and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: and whosoever beareth away aught of the carcase of them, shall wash his clothes and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: Every animal that has divided hoofs but is not cleft-footed or does not chew the cud is unclean for you; everyone who touches them shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: As for every kind of beast which, though it parteth the hoof, yet is not cloven-footed nor cheweth the cud, unclean, they are unto you,—every one who toucheth them shall be unclean.

text.NRSV: All that walk on their paws, among the animals that walk on all fours, are unclean for you; whoever touches their carcass shall be unclean until the evening,
text.Rotherham: And, all that go upon their paws, among all the living things that go on all-fours, unclean, they are unto you,—whoso toucheth the carcase of them, shall be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: and the one who carries their carcass shall wash his clothes and be unclean until the evening; they are unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: And, he that beareth away the carcase of them shall wash his clothes, and shall be unclean until the evening,—unclean, they are unto you.

text.NRSV: These are unclean for you among the creatures that swarm upon the earth: the weasel, the mouse, the great lizard according to its kind,
text.Rotherham: And, these, unto you, shall be unclean, among the creeping things that creep upon the earth,—the weasel and the mouse, and the lizard after its kind;

text.NRSV: the gecko, the land crocodile, the lizard, the sand lizard, and the chameleon.
text.Rotherham: and the ferret and the chameleon and the wall-lizard,—and the winding lizard, and the mole.

text.NRSV: These are unclean for you among all that swarm; whoever touches them when they are dead shall be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: These, are they which are unclean to you among all that creep,—whosoever toucheth them when they are dead shall be unclean until the evening;

text.NRSV: And anything upon which any of them falls when they are dead shall be unclean, whether an article of wood or cloth or skin or sacking, any article that is used for any purpose; it shall be dipped into water, and it shall be unclean until the evening, and then it shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: and, everything whereon any of them shall fall when they are dead shall be unclean—of any articles of wood, or cloth, or skin, or sackcloth, any article wherewith any work is done,—shall be put in water and shall be unclean until the evening, and then be clean,

text.NRSV: And if any of them falls into any earthen vessel, all that is in it shall be unclean, and you shall break the vessel.
text.Rotherham: And, as for any earthen vessel whereinto any of them may fall, everything therein shall be unclean, and, the vessel itself, shall ye break in pieces.

text.NRSV: Any food in it which may be eaten, upon which water falls, shall be unclean; all drink that may be drunk from any such vessel shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: Of all the food that may be eaten, that whereon shall come water, shall be unclean,—and, all drink which might be drunk in any [such] vessel, shall be unclean.

text.NRSV: Anything on which any part of the carcass falls shall be unclean; whether an oven or stove, it shall be broken in pieces; they are unclean, and shall remain unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: And, everything whereon shall fall any part of the carcase of them shall be unclean, oven or fire-range, it shall be destroyed, unclean, they are,—and, unclean, Shall they remain to you.

text.NRSV: But a spring or a cistern holding water shall be clean, while whatever touches the carcass in it shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: Notwithstanding, a fountain or cistern wherein is a gathering of waters shall be clean,—but, he that toucheth the carcase of them shall be unclean.

text.NRSV: If any part of their carcass falls upon any seed set aside for sowing, it is clean;
text.Rotherham: And when any part of the carcase of them shall fall upon seed for sowing, which is to be sown, the same is, clean.

text.NRSV: but if water is put on the seed and any part of their carcass falls on it, it is unclean for you.
text.Rotherham: But, when water shall be put upon seed, and there shall fall thereon any part of the carcase of them, unclean, it is to you.

text.NRSV: If any animal of which you may eat dies, anyone who touches its carcass shall be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, when any of the beasts which are yours for food shall die, he that toucheth the carcase of it shall be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: Those who eat of its carcass shall wash their clothes and be unclean until the evening; those who carry the carcass shall wash their clothes and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, he that eateth of the carcase of it, shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the evening,—he also that carrieth away the carcase thereof shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: All creatures that swarm upon the earth are detestable; they shall not be eaten.
text.Rotherham: And, any creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, an abomination, it is—it shall not be eaten.

text.NRSV: Whatever moves on its belly, and whatever moves on all fours, or whatever has many feet, all the creatures that swarm upon the earth, you shall not eat; for they are detestable.
text.Rotherham: Everything that goeth upon the belly, and everything that goeth upon all-fours, even to everything having many feet, as regardeth any creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, ye shall not eat them for, an abomination, they are.

text.NRSV: You shall not make yourselves detestable with any creature that swarms; you shall not defile yourselves with them, and so become unclean.
text.Rotherham: Do not make your persons abominable, with any creeping thing that creepeth,—neither shall ye make yourselves unclean with them, that ye should become unclean thereby.

text.NRSV: For I am the Lord your God; sanctify yourselves therefore, and be holy, for I am holy. You shall not defile yourselves with any swarming creature that moves on the earth.
text.Rotherham: For, I—Yahweh, am your God, therefore shall ye hallow yourselves and remain holy; for, holy, am, I,—therefore shall ye not make your persons unclean, with any creeping thing that crawleth upon the earth;

text.NRSV: For I am the Lord who brought you up from the land of Egypt, to be your God; you shall be holy, for I am holy.
text.Rotherham: for, I—Yahweh, am he that brought you up out of the land of Egypt, that I might become your God,—therefore shall ye be holy, for, holy, am, I.

text.NRSV: This is the law of animals and birds and all living creatures that move in the waters and all creatures that swarm on the ground,
text.Rotherham: This, is the law of beast and of bird, and of every living soul that moveth in the waters,—and as to every soul that creepeth upon the earth:

text.NRSV: to make a distinction between the unclean and the clean, and between the living creature that may be eaten and the living creature that may not be eaten.
text.Rotherham: That ye may make a difference—between the unclean and the clean,—and between the living thing that may be eaten, and the living thing which may not be eaten.

text.NRSV: The Lord spoke to Moses, saying:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses, saying:

text.NRSV: Speak to the people of Israel, saying: If a woman conceives and bears a male child, she shall be ceremonially unclean seven days; as at the time of her menstruation, she shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: Speak unto the sons of Israel, saying, A woman when she conceiveth seed and giveth birth to a male child, then shall she be unclean seven days, according to the days of her removal in her sickness shall she be unclean.

text.NRSV: On the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.
text.Rotherham: And, on the eighth day, shall the flesh of his foreskin be circumcised.

text.NRSV: Her time of blood purification shall be thirty-three days; she shall not touch any holy thing, or come into the sanctuary, until the days of her purification are completed.
text.Rotherham: And, for thirty-three days, shall she continue in the blood of purification,—no hallowed thing, shall she touch, and, into the sanctuary, shall she not enter, until her days of purification are fulfilled.

text.NRSV: If she bears a female child, she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her menstruation; her time of blood purification shall be sixty-six days.
text.Rotherham: But if, a female child, she bear, then shall she be unclean two weeks as in her removal,—and, for sixty-six days, shall she continue in the blood of purification.

text.NRSV: When the days of her purification are completed, whether for a son or for a daughter, she shall bring to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting a lamb in its first year for a burnt offering, and a pigeon or a turtledove for a sin offering.
text.Rotherham: And, when the days of her purification are fulfilled, whether for a son or for a daughter, she shall bring in a lamb, the choice of its year, as an ascending-sacrifice, and a young pigeon or a turtle-dove, as a sin-bearer, unto the entrance of the tent of meeting, unto the priest;

text.NRSV: He shall offer it before the Lord, and make atonement on her behalf; then she shall be clean from her flow of blood. This is the law for her who bears a child, male or female.
text.Rotherham: and he shall bring it near before Yahweh so shall the priest put a propitiatory-covering over her, and she shall be clean from her fountain of blood. This, is the law for her that hath given birth, to a male child or to a female.

text.NRSV: If she cannot afford a sheep, she shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons, one for a burnt offering and the other for a sin offering; and the priest shall make atonement on her behalf, and she shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: But, if her hand findeth not sufficiency for a lamb, then shall she take two turtle doves or two young pigeons, one for an ascending-sacrifice and one for a sin-bearer,—so shall the priest put a propitiatory-covering over her, and she shall be clean.

text.NRSV: The Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying—

text.NRSV: When a person has on the skin of his body a swelling or an eruption or a spot, and it turns into a leprous disease on the skin of his body, he shall be brought to Aaron the priest or to one of his sons the priests.
text.Rotherham: When, any man, shall have—in the skin of his flesh—a rising, or a scab, or a bright spot, and it shall become in the skin of his flesh the plague-spot of leprosy, then shall he be brought in unto Aaron the priest, or unto one of his sons the priests;

text.NRSV: The priest shall examine the disease on the skin of his body, and if the hair in the diseased area has turned white and the disease appears to be deeper than the skin of his body, it is a leprous disease; after the priest has examined him he shall pronounce him ceremonially unclean.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall view the spot in the skin of his flesh—if, the hair in the plague, have turned white and the appearance of the spot be deeper than the skin of his flesh, the plague-spot of leprosy, it is,—so the priest shall view him and pronounce him unclean.

text.NRSV: But if the spot is white in the skin of his body, and appears no deeper than the skin, and the hair in it has not turned white, the priest shall confine the diseased person for seven days.
text.Rotherham: But, if the bright spot, though white in the skin of his flesh, is not deeper in appearance than the skin, and, the hair, hath not turned white, then shall the priest shut up the plagued one, seven days.

text.NRSV: The priest shall examine him on the seventh day, and if in his eyes the disease is checked and the disease has not spread in the skin, the priest shall confine him seven days more.
text.Rotherham: And the priest shall view him on the seventh day, and lo! if the spot hath stayed to his sight, and the spot hath not spread in the skin, then shall the priest shut him up seven days more,

text.NRSV: The priest shall examine him again on the seventh day, and if the disease has abated and the disease has not spread in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him clean; it is only an eruption; and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean.
text.Rotherham: Then shall the priest view him on the seventh day, a second time, and lo! if the spot is, faint, and the spot hath not spread in the skin, then shall the priest pronounce him clean—it is, a scab, and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean.

text.NRSV: But if the eruption spreads in the skin after he has shown himself to the priest for his cleansing, he shall appear again before the priest.
text.Rotherham: But if the scab shall have, verily spread, in the skin, since he was shown to the priest that he might be cleansed, then shall he shew himself again unto the priest;

text.NRSV: The priest shall make an examination, and if the eruption has spread in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is a leprous disease.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall take a view, and lo! if the scab hath spread in the skin, then shall the priest pronounce him unclean—leprosy, it is.

text.NRSV: When a person has a leprous disease, he shall be brought to the priest.
text.Rotherham: When, the plague of leprosy, cometh to be in any human being, then shall he be brought in unto the priest;

text.NRSV: The priest shall make an examination, and if there is a white swelling in the skin that has turned the hair white, and there is quick raw flesh in the swelling,
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall take a view, and lo! if there is a white rising in the skin and, the same, hath turned the hair white,—and there be a wound of raw flesh in the rising,

text.NRSV: it is a chronic leprous disease in the skin of his body. The priest shall pronounce him unclean; he shall not confine him, for he is unclean.
text.Rotherham: an old leprosy, it is in the skin of his flesh, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean,—he shall not shut him up, for unclean, he is.

text.NRSV: But if the disease breaks out in the skin, so that it covers all the skin of the diseased person from head to foot, so far as the priest can see,
text.Rotherham: But, if the leprosy, cometh quite out, in the skin, and the leprosy covereth all the skin of him that, is plagued, from his head even unto his feet,—so far as appeareth to the eyes of the priest,

text.NRSV: the priest shall make an examination, and if the disease has covered all his body, he shall pronounce him clean of the disease; since it has all turned white, he is clean.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest takes view and lo! if the leprosy hath covered all his flesh, then shall he pronounce clean him that was plagued,—all of it, hath turned white, clean, he is.

text.NRSV: But if raw flesh ever appears on him, he shall be unclean;
text.Rotherham: But, the very day there appeareth in him raw flesh, he shall be unclean;

text.NRSV: the priest shall examine the raw flesh and pronounce him unclean. Raw flesh is unclean, for it is a leprous disease.
text.Rotherham: so then the priest shall view the raw flesh, and pronounce him unclean,—as for the raw flesh, unclean, it is, leprosy, it is.

text.NRSV: But if the raw flesh again turns white, he shall come to the priest;
text.Rotherham: Or, if the raw flesh turn again and be changed to white, then shall he come in unto the priest;

text.NRSV: the priest shall examine him, and if the disease has turned white, the priest shall pronounce the diseased person clean. He is clean.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall view him, and lo! if the spot hath changed to white, then shall the priest pronounce clean him that was plagued—clean, he is.

text.NRSV: When a boil appears on the skin of one’s body and it heals,
text.Rotherham: And, when, any one’s flesh, hath, in the skin thereof, a boil,—and then it is healed;

text.NRSV: and in the place of the boil there appears a white swelling or a reddish-white spot, it shall be shown to the priest.
text.Rotherham: but in the place of the boil, is a white rising, or a bright spot, reddish white, then shall it be shown unto the priest.

text.NRSV: The priest shall make an examination, and if it appears deeper than the skin and its hair has turned white, the priest shall pronounce him unclean; this is a leprous disease, broken out in the boil.
text.Rotherham: And the priest shall take a view and lo! if the appearance thereof, is lower than the skin, and, the hair thereof, hath turned white, then shall the priest pronounce him unclean—the plague-spot of leprosy, it, is, in the boil, broken out.

text.NRSV: But if the priest examines it and the hair on it is not white, nor is it deeper than the skin but has abated, the priest shall confine him seven days.
text.Rotherham: But, if the priest shall view it and lo! there is no white hair therein, and it is not deeper than the skin, and, in itself, is faint, then shall the priest shut him up seven days;

text.NRSV: If it spreads in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is diseased.
text.Rotherham: and, if it, clearly spreadeth, in the skin, then shall the priest pronounce him unclean—a plague-spot, it is.

text.NRSV: But if the spot remains in one place and does not spread, it is the scar of the boil; the priest shall pronounce him clean.
text.Rotherham: But if in its place the bright spot stayeth hath not spread, a boil, it is,—and the priest shall pronounce him clean.

text.NRSV: Or, when the body has a burn on the skin and the raw flesh of the burn becomes a spot, reddish-white or white,
text.Rotherham: Or, when, any one’s flesh, hath in the skin thereof a fiery burning,—and the burning wound becometh a bright spot reddish white, or white,

text.NRSV: the priest shall examine it. If the hair in the spot has turned white and it appears deeper than the skin, it is a leprous disease; it has broken out in the burn, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean. This is a leprous disease.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest view it—and lo! if the hair is turned white in the bright spot and the appearance thereof is deeper than the skin, leprosy, it is, broken out, in the burning,—so the priest shall pronounce him unclean, the plague-spot of leprosy, it is.

text.NRSV: But if the priest examines it and the hair on the spot is not white, and it is no deeper than the skin but has abated, the priest shall confine him seven days.
text.Rotherham: But, if the priest shall view it and lo! there is not, in the bright spot white hair, and it is not deeper than the skin but, itself, is faint, then shall the priest shut him up seven days;

text.NRSV: The priest shall examine him the seventh day; if it is spreading in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him unclean. This is a leprous disease.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall view him on the seventh day,—if it, hath plainly spread in the skin, then shall the priest pronounce him unclean, the plague-spot of leprosy, it is.

text.NRSV: But if the spot remains in one place and does not spread in the skin but has abated, it is a swelling from the burn, and the priest shall pronounce him clean; for it is the scar of the burn.
text.Rotherham: But, if, in its place the bright spot hath stayed, and hath not spread in the skin, but, itself, is faint, the rising of a burning, it is,—and the priest shall pronounce him clean, for only the inflaming of the burning, it is.

text.NRSV: When a man or woman has a disease on the head or in the beard,
text.Rotherham: And, when there cometh to be in, any man or woman, a spot,—in the head or in the beard,

text.NRSV: the priest shall examine the disease. If it appears deeper than the skin and the hair in it is yellow and thin, the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is a scall, a leprous disease of the head or the beard.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest view the spot and lo! if, the appearance thereof, is deeper than the skin, and, therein, is yellow, thin hair, then shall the priest pronounce him unclean a scall, it is, a leprosy of the head or of the beard, it is.

text.NRSV: But if the priest examines the scaly disease, and it does not appear deeper than the skin and there is no black hair in it, the priest shall confine the person with the scaly disease for seven days.
text.Rotherham: But when the priest vieweth the spot, and lo! there is, no appearance, of it deeper than the skin, and, no dark hair, is therein, then shall the priest shut up him that hath the plague-spot of scall, seven days;

text.NRSV: On the seventh day the priest shall examine the itch; if the scall has not spread, and there is no yellow hair in it, and the scall does not appear deeper than the skin,
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall view the spot on the seventh day, and lo! if the scall hath not spread, and there hath not come to be therein yellow hair,—and, the appearance of the scall, is not deeper than the skin,

text.NRSV: he shall shave, but the scall he shall not shave. The priest shall confine the person with the scall for seven days more.
text.Rotherham: then shall he shave himself, but the scall, shall he not shave, and the priest shall shut up him who hath the scall seven days, more;

text.NRSV: On the seventh day the priest shall examine the scall; if the scall has not spread in the skin and it appears no deeper than the skin, the priest shall pronounce him clean; he shall wash his clothes, and be clean.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest view the scall, on the seventh day, and lo! if the scall hath not spread in the skin, and, the appearance thereof, is not deeper than the skin, then shall the priest pronounce him clean, and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean.

text.NRSV: But if the scall spreads in the skin after he has been pronounced clean,
text.Rotherham: But, if the scall do indeed spread in the skin,—after he hath been pronounced clean,

text.NRSV: the priest shall examine him. If the scall has spread in the skin, the priest need not seek for yellow hair; he is unclean.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest take a view, and lo! if the scall hath spread in the skin, the priest shall not search for the yellow hair—unclean, he is.

text.NRSV: If in his eyes the scall is unchanged and black hair has grown in it, the scall is healed; he is clean; and the priest shall pronounce him clean.
text.Rotherham: But, if in his eyes, the scall is at a stay, and dark hair hath grown up therein, the scall is healed, clean, he is,—and the priest shall pronounce him clean.

text.NRSV: When a man or a woman has spots on the skin of the body, white spots,
text.Rotherham: And when either, man or woman, hath in the skin of their flesh bright spots,—bright spots that are white,

text.NRSV: the priest shall make an examination, and if the spots on the skin of the body are of a dull white, it is a rash that has broken out on the skin; he is clean.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest take a view and lo! if, in the skin of their flesh, are bright spots that are dull white, dead white spot, it is, that hath broken through in the skin—clean, he is.

text.NRSV: If anyone loses the hair from his head, he is bald but he is clean.
text.Rotherham: And, when, any man’s, head loseth its hair, though, bald, he is, clean.

text.NRSV: If he loses the hair from his forehead and temples, he has baldness of the forehead but he is clean.
text.Rotherham: And if, in front, his head loseth its hair, though bald in the forehead, he is, clean.

text.NRSV: But if there is on the bald head or the bald forehead a reddish-white diseased spot, it is a leprous disease breaking out on his bald head or his bald forehead.
text.Rotherham: But, should there be, in the baldness behind, or in the baldness in front, a spot that is reddish white, leprosy broken out, it is, in his baldness behind, or in his baldness in front.

text.NRSV: The priest shall examine him; if the diseased swelling is reddish-white on his bald head or on his bald forehead, which resembles a leprous disease in the skin of the body,
text.Rotherham: So the priest shall view it, and lo! if, the rising-spot, be reddish white in his baldness behind or in his baldness in front,—like the appearance of leprosy in the skin of the flesh,

text.NRSV: he is leprous, he is unclean. The priest shall pronounce him unclean; the disease is on his head.
text.Rotherham: a leprous man, is he, unclean, he is,—unclean, shall the priest pronounce him in his head, is his plague.

text.NRSV: The person who has the leprous disease shall wear torn clothes and let the hair of his head be disheveled; and he shall cover his upper lip and cry out, “Unclean, unclean.”
text.Rotherham: Now, as for the leper in whom is the plague, His clothes, shall be rent, And, his head, shall be bare, And, his beard, shall he cover,—And, Unclean! Unclean! shall he cry.

text.NRSV: He shall remain unclean as long as he has the disease; he is unclean. He shall live alone; his dwelling shall be outside the camp.
text.Rotherham: All the days that the plague is in him, shall he continue unclean, Unclean, he is,—Alone, shall he remain, Outside the camp, shall be his dwelling.

text.NRSV: When a garment has a mark of leprosy in it, whether it is a woolen or a linen garment,
text.Rotherham: And, when, in a garment, there is a plague-spot of leprosy,—whether in a garment of wool, or a garment of flax;

text.NRSV: in warp or woof of linen or wool, or in skin or in anything made of skin,
text.Rotherham: either in warp or in weft, made with flax, or with wool,—or in a skin, or in anything wrought of skin;

text.NRSV: if the disease shows greenish or reddish in the garment, whether in warp or woof or in skin or in anything made of skin, it is a leprous disease and shall be shown to the priest.
text.Rotherham: and the spot cometh to be of a greenish yellow or reddish, in the garment or in the skin, whether in warp or in weft, or in any utensil of skin, the plague-spot of leprosy, it is,—and shall be shown unto the priest;

text.NRSV: The priest shall examine the disease, and isolate the disease seven days.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall view the spot,—and shall shut up him that is plagued seven days;

text.NRSV: If the disease has spread in the garment, in warp or woof, or in the skin, whatever be the use of the skin, this is a spreading leprous disease; it is unclean.
text.Rotherham: then shall he view the spot, on the seventh day if the spot hath spread in the garment whether in warp or in weft, or in the skin, or anything which may be made of skin for service, the spot is a fretting leprosy, unclean, it is.

text.NRSV: He shall burn the garment, whether diseased in warp or woof, woolen or linen, or anything of skin, for it is a spreading leprous disease; it shall be burned in fire.
text.Rotherham: Then shall he burn up the garment whether it be in the warp or the weft in wool or in flax, or any utensil of skin, wherein shall be the plague-spot,—for, a fretting leprosy, it is, in fire, shall it be burnt up.

text.NRSV: If the priest makes an examination, and the disease has not spread in the garment, in warp or woof or in anything of skin,
text.Rotherham: But if the priest shall take a view, and lo! the spot has not spread, in the garment, either in warp or in weft,—or in any utensil of skin,

text.NRSV: the priest shall command that the diseased article be washed, and he shall isolate it seven days more.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest give command, and they shall wash that wherein is the spot,—and he shall shut it up seven days more;

text.NRSV: Then the priest shall examine the diseased article after it has been washed. If the spot has not changed color, though the disease has not spread, it is unclean; you shall burn it in fire, whether the leprous spot is on the inside or on the outside.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest take a view after the plagued garment hath been washed and lo! if the spot hath not changed its look, then though the spot hath not spread, yet unclean, it is, in the fire, shalt thou burn it up,—a sunken spot, it is, in the back thereof, or in the front thereof.

text.NRSV: If the priest makes an examination, and the disease has abated after it is washed, he shall tear the spot out of the garment, in skin, or in warp or woof.
text.Rotherham: And, if the priest hath taken a view, and lo! the spot is, faint, since it hath been washed, then shall he rend it out of the garment, or out of the skin, whether out of the warp or out of the weft;

text.NRSV: If it appears again in the garment, in warp or woof, or in anything of skin, it is spreading; you shall burn with fire that in which is the disease.
text.Rotherham: and, if it appear still in the garment—either in the warp or in the weft, or in any utensil of skin, a breaking out, it is,—in the fire, shalt thou burn up that wherein is the plague.

text.NRSV: But the garment, warp or woof, or anything of skin, from which the disease disappears when you have washed it, shall then be washed a second time, and it shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: But, as for the garment—whether the warp or the weft or any utensil of skin which thou shalt wash, and the plague shall depart therefrom, then shall it be washed a second time, and shall be clean.

text.NRSV: This is the rule for leprous diseases in a garment of wool or linen, either in warp or woof, or in anything of skin, for pronouncing it clean or unclean.
text.Rotherham: This, is the law as to the plague-spot of leprosy, in a garment of wool or of flax, whether in the warp or the weft, or in any utensil of skin,—To pronounce it clean, or to pronounce it unclean.

text.NRSV: The Lord spoke to Moses, saying:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses, saying—

text.NRSV: This shall be the ritual for the leprous person at the time of his cleansing: He shall be brought to the priest;
text.Rotherham: This, shall be the law of the leper, in the day when he is declared clean,—he shall be brought in unto the priest;

text.NRSV: the priest shall go out of the camp, and the priest shall make an examination. If the disease is healed in the leprous person,
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest go forth unto the outside of the camp,—and the priest shall take a view, and lo! if the plague of leprosy hath been healed out of the leper,;

text.NRSV: the priest shall command that two living clean birds and cedarwood and crimson yarn and hyssop be brought for the one who is to be cleansed.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest give command,—and there shall be taken—for him that is to be cleansed—two living clean birds,—and cedar wood, and crimson am hyssop;

text.NRSV: The priest shall command that one of the birds be slaughtered over fresh water in an earthen vessel.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall give command, and one bird shall be slain,—within an earthen vessel over living water:

text.NRSV: He shall take the living bird with the cedarwood and the crimson yarn and the hyssop, and dip them and the living bird in the blood of the bird that was slaughtered over the fresh water.
text.Rotherham: as for the living bird, he shall take, it, and the cedar wood and the crimson, and the hyssop,—and shall dip them and the living bird, in the blood of the bird that hath been slain over the living water;

text.NRSV: He shall sprinkle it seven times upon the one who is to be cleansed of the leprous disease; then he shall pronounce him clean, and he shall let the living bird go into the open field.
text.Rotherham: and shall sprinkle upon him that is to be cleansed from the leprosy, seven times,—and shall pronounce him clean, and shall let go the living bird over the face of the field.

text.NRSV: The one who is to be cleansed shall wash his clothes, and shave off all his hair, and bathe himself in water, and he shall be clean. After that he shall come into the camp, but shall live outside his tent seven days.
text.Rotherham: And he that is to be declared clean shall wash his clothes and shave off all his hair, and bathe in water, and be clean, and, afterwards, shall he come into the camp,—and dwell outside his tent, seven days;

text.NRSV: On the seventh day he shall shave all his hair: of head, beard, eyebrows; he shall shave all his hair. He shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and he shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: and it shall be on the seventh day, that he shall shave off all the hair of his head, and his beard, and his eyebrows, even all his hair, shall he shave off,—and shall wash his clothes and bathe his flesh in water so shall he be clean.

text.NRSV: On the eighth day he shall take two male lambs without blemish, and one ewe lamb in its first year without blemish, and a grain offering of three-tenths of an ephah of choice flour mixed with oil, and one log of oil.
text.Rotherham: And, on the eighth day, he shall take two he-lambs, without defect, and one ewe-lamb, the choice of its year without defect,—and three-tenths of fine meal for a meal-offering, overflowed with oil, and one log of oil.

text.NRSV: The priest who cleanses shall set the person to be cleansed, along with these things, before the Lord, at the entrance of the tent of meeting.
text.Rotherham: And the priest that is cleansing him shall cause the man that is to be cleansed, and those things to stand before Yahweh, at the opening of the tent of meeting.

text.NRSV: The priest shall take one of the male lambs, and offer it as a guilt offering, along with the log of oil, and raise them as an elevation offering before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: And the priest shall take one he-lamb, and bring him near as a guilt-bearer and the log of oil,—and shall wave them as a wave-offering, before Yahweh;

text.NRSV: He shall slaughter the male lamb in the place where the sin offering and the burnt offering are slaughtered in the holy place; for the guilt offering, like the sin offering, belongs to the priest: it is most holy.
text.Rotherham: and shall slay the lamb in the place where the sin-bearer and the ascending-sacrifice are slain, in the holy place,—for like the sin-bearer, the guilt-bearer, is the priest’s, most holy, it is.

text.NRSV: The priest shall take some of the blood of the guilt offering and put it on the lobe of the right ear of the one to be cleansed, and on the thumb of the right hand, and on the big toe of the right foot.
text.Rotherham: Then shall the priest take of the blood of the guilt-bearer, and the priest shall put it upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed,—and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot;

text.NRSV: The priest shall take some of the log of oil and pour it into the palm of his own left hand,
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall take of the log of oil,—and shall pour it upon the palm of the priest’s left hand,

text.NRSV: and dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand and sprinkle some oil with his finger seven times before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall dip his right finger [and take] of the oil that is on the palm of his left hand,—and shall sprinkle of the oil with his finger seven times before Yahweh:

text.NRSV: Some of the oil that remains in his hand the priest shall put on the lobe of the right ear of the one to be cleansed, and on the thumb of the right hand, and on the big toe of the right foot, on top of the blood of the guilt offering.
text.Rotherham: and of the remainder of the oil which is on the palm of his hand, shall the priest put upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot,—upon the blood of the guilt-bearer;

text.NRSV: The rest of the oil that is in the priest’s hand he shall put on the head of the one to be cleansed. Then the priest shall make atonement on his behalf before the Lord:
text.Rotherham: and that which remaineth of the oil that is on the palm of the priest’s hand, he shall put upon the head of him that is to be cleansed,—so shall the priest put a propitiatory-covering over him, before Yahweh.

text.NRSV: the priest shall offer the sin offering, to make atonement for the one to be cleansed from his uncleanness. Afterward he shall slaughter the burnt offering;
text.Rotherham: Then shall the priest offer the sin-bearer, and shall put a propitiatory-covering over him that is to be cleansed because of his uncleanness,—and, afterwards, shall he slay the ascending-sacrifice.

text.NRSV: and the priest shall offer the burnt offering and the grain offering on the altar. Thus the priest shall make atonement on his behalf and he shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: And the priest shall cause the ascending-sacrifice, and the meal-offering to ascend at the altar,—so shall the priest put a propitiatory-covering over him, and he shall be clean.

text.NRSV: But if he is poor and cannot afford so much, he shall take one male lamb for a guilt offering to be elevated, to make atonement on his behalf, and one-tenth of an ephah of choice flour mixed with oil for a grain offering and a log of oil;
text.Rotherham: But if he be poor and his hand hath not enough, then shall he take one he-lamb as a guilt-bearer to be waved to put a propitiatory-covering over him,—and one-tenth of fine meal, overflowed with oil for a meal-offering, and a log of oil,

text.NRSV: also two turtledoves or two pigeons, such as he can afford, one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering.
text.Rotherham: and two turtle-doves or two young pigeons, for which his hand hath enough,—so shall one be a sin-bearer, and the other an ascending-sacrifice.

text.NRSV: On the eighth day he shall bring them for his cleansing to the priest, to the entrance of the tent of meeting, before the Lord;
text.Rotherham: And he shall bring them in on the eighth day, for his cleansing, unto the priest,—unto the entrance of the tent of meeting, before Yahweh.

text.NRSV: and the priest shall take the lamb of the guilt offering and the log of oil, and the priest shall raise them as an elevation offering before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: Then shall the priest take the guilt-bearing lamb and the log of oil,—and the priest shall wave them as a wave-offering, before Yahweh.

text.NRSV: He shall slaughter the lamb of the guilt offering; and the priest shall take some of the blood of the guilt offering, and put it on the lobe of the right ear of the one to be cleansed, and on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.
text.Rotherham: Then shall the guilt-bearing lamb be slain, and the priest shall take of the blood of the guilt-bearer, and put upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed,—and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot;

text.NRSV: The priest shall pour some of the oil into the palm of his own left hand,
text.Rotherham: also of the oil, shall the priest pour out,—on the palm of his own left hand;

text.NRSV: and shall sprinkle with his right finger some of the oil that is in his left hand seven times before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall sprinkle with his right finger, of the oil that is on the palm of his left hand,—seven times, before Yahweh;

text.NRSV: The priest shall put some of the oil that is in his hand on the lobe of the right ear of the one to be cleansed, and on the thumb of the right hand, and the big toe of the right foot, where the blood of the guilt offering was placed.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest put, of the oil that is on the palm of his hand, upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot,—upon the place of the blood of the guilt-offering;

text.NRSV: The rest of the oil that is in the priest’s hand he shall put on the head of the one to be cleansed, to make atonement on his behalf before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: and that which remaineth of the oil which is on the palm of the priest’s hand, shall he put upon the head of him that is to be cleansed,—to put a propitiatory-covering over him before Yahweh.

text.NRSV: And he shall offer, of the turtledoves or pigeons such as he can afford,
text.Rotherham: Then shall he offer one of the turtle-doves, or of the young pigeons,—of that for which his hand hath enough;

text.NRSV: one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering, along with a grain offering; and the priest shall make atonement before the Lord on behalf of the one being cleansed.
text.Rotherham: even that for which his hand hath enough,—the one as a sin-bearer and the other as an ascending-sacrifice, upon the meal-offering,—so shall the priest put a propitiatory-covering over him that is to be cleansed before Yahweh.

text.NRSV: This is the ritual for the one who has a leprous disease, who cannot afford the offerings for his cleansing.
text.Rotherham: This is the law for him in whom hath been the plague of leprosy, whose hand hath not enough for that which pertaineth to his cleansing.

text.NRSV: The Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses and unto Aaron saying:

text.NRSV: When you come into the land of Canaan, which I give you for a possession, and I put a leprous disease in a house in the land of your possession,
text.Rotherham: When ye enter into the land of Canaan which I am about to give you for a possession,—and I put a plague-mark of leprosy in a house, of the land of your possession,

text.NRSV: the owner of the house shall come and tell the priest, saying, “There seems to me to be some sort of disease in my house.”
text.Rotherham: then shall he that owneth the house come in, and tell the priest, saying,—A kind of plague-mark, appeareth to me in the house;

text.NRSV: The priest shall command that they empty the house before the priest goes to examine the disease, or all that is in the house will become unclean; and afterward the priest shall go in to inspect the house.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall give command, and they shall empty the house, ere yet the priest cometh in to view the mark, so that he do not pronounce unclean all that is in the house,—and, after this, shall the priest come in to view the house:

text.NRSV: He shall examine the disease; if the disease is in the walls of the house with greenish or reddish spots, and if it appears to be deeper than the surface,
text.Rotherham: then shall he view the mark and lo! if the mark is in the walls of the house, with sunken places greenish yellow or reddish,—and they appear to be lower than the [surface of the] wall,

text.NRSV: the priest shall go outside to the door of the house and shut up the house seven days.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest come forth out of the house, unto the entrance of the house,—and shall shut up the house seven days;

text.NRSV: The priest shall come again on the seventh day and make an inspection; if the disease has spread on the walls of the house,
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall return on the seventh day,—and take a view, and lo! if the mark hath spread in the walls of the house,

text.NRSV: the priest shall command that the stones in which the disease appears be taken out and thrown into an unclean place outside the city.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest give command, and they shall pull out the stones, wherein is the mark,—and cast them forth outside the city, into an unclean place;

text.NRSV: He shall have the inside of the house scraped thoroughly, and the plaster that is scraped off shall be dumped in an unclean place outside the city.
text.Rotherham: and, the house itself, shall he cause to be scraped on the inside round about,—and they shall pour out the mortar which they have scraped off, outside the city, into an unclean place;

text.NRSV: They shall take other stones and put them in the place of those stones, and take other plaster and plaster the house.
text.Rotherham: and shall take other stones, and put in the place of the stones,—and, other mortar, shall he take and plaster the house.

text.NRSV: If the disease breaks out again in the house, after he has taken out the stones and scraped the house and plastered it,
text.Rotherham: But if the mark again breaketh out in the house, after the taking out of the stones,—and after the scraping of the house and after the plastering,;

text.NRSV: the priest shall go and make inspection; if the disease has spread in the house, it is a spreading leprous disease in the house; it is unclean.
text.Rotherham: then shall the priest come in, and take a view, and lo! if the mark hath spread in the house, a fretting leprosy, it is in the house unclean, it is,

text.NRSV: He shall have the house torn down, its stones and timber and all the plaster of the house, and taken outside the city to an unclean place.
text.Rotherham: and he shall pull down the house,—the stones thereof, and the timber thereof and all the mortar of the house,—and shall carry them forth outside the city, into an unclean place.

text.NRSV: All who enter the house while it is shut up shall be unclean until the evening;
text.Rotherham: And as for him that entereth into the house, all the days it is shut up, he shall be unclean until the evening;

text.NRSV: and all who sleep in the house shall wash their clothes; and all who eat in the house shall wash their clothes.
text.Rotherham: And, he that lieth in the house, shall wash his clothes, and he that eateth in the house shall wash his clothes.

text.NRSV: If the priest comes and makes an inspection, and the disease has not spread in the house after the house was plastered, the priest shall pronounce the house clean; the disease is healed.
text.Rotherham: But, though the priest do come, into the house, and take a view, yet lo! if the plague-mark hath not spread in the house after the house was plastered, then shall the priest pronounce the house clean, for, healed, is the plague.

text.NRSV: To cleanse the house he shall take two birds, with cedarwood and crimson yarn and hyssop,
text.Rotherham: Then shall he take to cleanse, the house, two birds,—and cedar wood, and crimson, and hyssop;

text.NRSV: and shall slaughter one of the birds over fresh water in an earthen vessel,
text.Rotherham: and shall kill one bird,—within an earthen vessel over living water;

text.NRSV: and shall take the cedarwood and the hyssop and the crimson yarn, along with the living bird, and dip them in the blood of the slaughtered bird and the fresh water, and sprinkle the house seven times.
text.Rotherham: and take the cedar wood and the hyssop, and the crimson and the living bird, and dip them in the blood of the slain bird, and in the living water,—and sprinkle the house seven times;

text.NRSV: Thus he shall cleanse the house with the blood of the bird, and with the fresh water, and with the living bird, and with the cedarwood and hyssop and crimson yarn;
text.Rotherham: and so cleanse the house, with the blood of the bird and with the living water,—and with the living bird, and with the cedar wood and with the hyssop, and with the crimson;

text.NRSV: and he shall let the living bird go out of the city into the open field. So he shall make atonement for the house, and it shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: and shall let go the living bird unto the outside of the city, unto the face of the field,—so shall he put a propitiatory-covering over the house and it shall be clean.

text.NRSV: This is the ritual for every leprous disease and scale,
text.Rotherham: This, is the law,—for every plague-mark of leprosy and for scall;

text.NRSV: for leprous diseases in clothing and houses,
text.Rotherham: and for garment leprosy, and for house [leprosy] ;

text.NRSV: and for swelling or eruption or spot,
text.Rotherham: and for rising, and for scab and for a bright spot;

text.NRSV: to determine when it is unclean and when it is clean. This is the ritual for leprous diseases.
text.Rotherham: to give instruction, on the day of declaring unclean, and on the day of declaring clean,—This is the law of leprosy.

text.NRSV: The Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses and unto Aaron saying:

text.NRSV: Speak to the people of Israel: When any man has a discharge from his member, his discharge makes him ceremonially unclean.
text.Rotherham: Speak unto the sons of Israel, and ye shall say unto them,—When, any man whatsoever, hath a flowing from his flesh, his flux, is, unclean.

text.NRSV: The uncleanness of his discharge is this: whether his member flows with his discharge, or his member is stopped from discharging, it is uncleanness for him.
text.Rotherham: And, this shall be his uncleanness in his flux,—whether his flesh is running with his flux or his flesh hath closed from his flux, his uncleanness, it is.

text.NRSV: Any bed on which the one with the discharge lies shall be unclean; and everything on which he sits shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: All the bed whereon he that hath the flux lieth, shall be unclean,—and, every piece of furniture whereon he sitteth, shall be unclean.

text.NRSV: Anyone who touches his bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And whosoever toucheth his bed shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: All who sit on anything on which the one with the discharge has sat shall wash their clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, he that sitteth on that whereon he that hath the flux hath sat, shall wash his clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: All who touch the body of the one with the discharge shall wash their clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, he that toucheth the flesh of him that hath the flux, shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening,

text.NRSV: If the one with the discharge spits on persons who are clean, then they shall wash their clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, when he that hath a flux spitteth on him that is clean, then shall he wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: Any saddle on which the one with the discharge rides shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: And, every saddle whereon he that hath the flux rideth, shall be unclean.

text.NRSV: All who touch anything that was under him shall be unclean until the evening, and all who carry such a thing shall wash their clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, whosoever toucheth anything that was under him shall be unclean until the evening; and, he that carrieth them shall wash his clothes and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: All those whom the one with the discharge touches without having rinsed his hands in water shall wash their clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, whomsoever he that hath the flux toucheth, not having rinsed, his hands in water, then shall he wash his clothes, and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening,

text.NRSV: Any earthen vessel that the one with the discharge touches shall be broken; and every vessel of wood shall be rinsed in water.
text.Rotherham: And the earthen vessel which he that hath the flux toucheth shall be broken in pieces,—and, every vessel of wood, shall be rinsed in water.

text.NRSV: When the one with a discharge is cleansed of his discharge, he shall count seven days for his cleansing; he shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in fresh water, and he shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: And when he that hath the flux becometh clean from his flux, then shall he number to himself seven days for his cleansing, and wash his clothes,—and bathe his flesh in living water, and be clean.

text.NRSV: On the eighth day he shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons and come before the Lord to the entrance of the tent of meeting and give them to the priest.
text.Rotherham: And, on the eighth day, he shall take for himself two turtle doves or two young pigeons,—and come in before Yahweh, unto the entrance of the tent of meeting, and give them to the priest;

text.NRSV: The priest shall offer them, one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make atonement on his behalf before the Lord for his discharge.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall offer them, one as a sin-bearer and the other as an ascending-sacrifice,—so shall the priest put a propitiatory-covering over him, before Yahweh, because of his flux.

text.NRSV: If a man has an emission of semen, he shall bathe his whole body in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, when there goeth out from, any man, an outflow of seed, then shall he bathe all his flesh in water, and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: Everything made of cloth or of skin on which the semen falls shall be washed with water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, in the case of any garment or any skin whereupon there shall come to be an outflow of seed, then shall it be washed in water, and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: If a man lies with a woman and has an emission of semen, both shall bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: Also, a woman with whom man lieth carnally, then shall they bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: When a woman has a discharge of blood that is her regular discharge from her body, she shall be in her impurity for seven days, and whoever touches her shall be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: And, when a, woman, hath a flow, and her flow in her flesh is, blood, seven days, shall she continue in her removal, and whosoever toucheth her, shall be unclean until the evening;

text.NRSV: Everything upon which she lies during her impurity shall be unclean; everything also upon which she sits shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: and, whatsoever she lieth upon in her removal, shall be unclean,—and, whatsoever she sitteth upon, shall be unclean;

text.NRSV: Whoever touches her bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: and whosoever toucheth her bed, shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening;

text.NRSV: Whoever touches anything upon which she sits shall wash his clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening;
text.Rotherham: and, whosoever toucheth any thing whereon she sitteth shall wash his clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening;

text.NRSV: whether it is the bed or anything upon which she sits, when he touches it he shall be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: and, whether, on her bed, it is, or on any thing whereon she hath been sitting, when he toucheth it, he shall be unclean until the evening;

text.NRSV: If any man lies with her, and her impurity falls on him, he shall be unclean seven days; and every bed on which he lies shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: and if man shall even lie with her, and her cause for removal be upon him, then shall he be unclean seven days,—and, all the bed whereon he shall lie, shall be unclean.

text.NRSV: If a woman has a discharge of blood for many days, not at the time of her impurity, or if she has a discharge beyond the time of her impurity, all the days of her discharge she shall continue in uncleanness; as in the days of her impurity, she shall be unclean.
text.Rotherham: And when any woman’s, flow of blood lasteth many day, outside the time of her removal, or when it floweth beyond her removal, all the days of her unclean flow, shall she be as in the days of her removal—unclean, she is.

text.NRSV: Every bed on which she lies during all the days of her discharge shall be treated as the bed during her impurity; and everything on which she sits shall be unclean, as in the uncleanness of her impurity.
text.Rotherham: All the bed whereon she lieth during all the days of her flow, like her bed in her removal, shall be to her,—and, every thing whereon she sitteth, shall be, unclean, like the uncleanness in her removal;

text.NRSV: Whoever touches these things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.
text.Rotherham: and whosoever toucheth them, shall be unclean,—and shall wash his clothes, and bathe in water, and be unclean until the evening.

text.NRSV: If she is cleansed of her discharge, she shall count seven days, and after that she shall be clean.
text.Rotherham: But, if she be clean from her flow, then shall she count to herself seven days and afterwards, shall she count herself clean.

text.NRSV: On the eighth day she shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons and bring them to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting.
text.Rotherham: And, on the eighth day, shall she take to herself two turtle doves, or two young pigeons,—and bring them in unto the priest, unto the entrance of the tent of meeting;

text.NRSV: The priest shall offer one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make atonement on her behalf before the Lord for her unclean discharge.
text.Rotherham: and the priest shall offer the one as a sin-bearer, and the other as an ascending-sacrifice,—so shall the priest put a propitiatory-covering over her, before Yahweh, because of her unclean flow.

text.NRSV: Thus you shall keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness, so that they do not die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst.
text.Rotherham: Thus shall ye warn the sons of Israel from their uncleanness, And they shall not die in their uncleanness, By reason of their making unclean my habitation which is in their midst.

text.NRSV: This is the ritual for those who have a discharge: for him who has an emission of semen, becoming unclean thereby,
text.Rotherham: This, is the law—Of him that hath a flux,—And of him from whom goeth an outflow of seed, making unclean thereby;

text.NRSV: for her who is in the infirmity of her period, for the man or woman who has a discharge, and for the man who lies with a woman who is unclean.
text.Rotherham: And of her that is unwell with her cause for removal, And of him whose flux floweth, For the male, and for the female,—And for a man who lieth with her that is unclean.

text.NRSV: The Lord spoke to Moses, after the death of the two sons of Aaron, when they drew near before the Lord and died.
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses, after the death of the two sons of Aaron,—when they had offered strange fire before Yahweh and died.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Moses: Tell your brother Aaron not to come just at any time into the sanctuary inside the curtain before the mercy seat that is upon the ark, or he will die; for I appear in the cloud upon the mercy seat.
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto Moses—Speak unto Aaron thy brother, that he do not come at all times into the holy place, within the veil,—into the presence of the propitiatory, which is upon the ark, so shall he not die, for, in the cloud, will I appear upon the propitiatory.

text.NRSV: Thus shall Aaron come into the holy place: with a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering.
text.Rotherham: Herewith, shall Aaron come into the holy place,—With a bullock the choice of the herd as a sin-bearer, and a ram as an ascending-sacrifice.

text.NRSV: He shall put on the holy linen tunic, and shall have the linen undergarments next to his body, fasten the linen sash, and wear the linen turban; these are the holy vestments. He shall bathe his body in water, and then put them on.
text.Rotherham: A holy tunic of linen, shall he put on and drawers of linen, shall be upon his flesh, and with a band of linen, shall he gird himself, and with a turban of linen, shall his head be wrapped about,—holy garments, they are, therefore shall he bathe in water his flesh, and so put them on.

text.NRSV: He shall take from the congregation of the people of Israel two male goats for a sin offering, and one ram for a burnt offering.
text.Rotherham: And, from the assembly of the sons of Israel, shall he receive two he-goats, for bearing sin,—and one ram, as an ascending-sacrifice.

text.NRSV: Aaron shall offer the bull as a sin offering for himself, and shall make atonement for himself and for his house.
text.Rotherham: Then shall Aaron bring near the sin-bearing bullock which is for himself,—and put a propitiatory-covering about himself, and about his household.

text.NRSV: He shall take the two goats and set them before the Lord at the entrance of the tent of meeting;
text.Rotherham: Then shall he take the two goats,—and cause them to stand before Yahweh, at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

text.NRSV: and Aaron shall cast lots on the two goats, one lot for the Lord and the other lot for Azazel.
text.Rotherham: And Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats,—one lot for Yahweh, and one lot for Azazel.

text.NRSV: Aaron shall present the goat on which the lot fell for the Lord, and offer it as a sin offering;
text.Rotherham: Then shall Aaron bring near the goat over which came up the lot for Yahweh,—and shall make of him a sin-bearer;

text.NRSV: but the goat on which the lot fell for Azazel shall be presented alive before the Lord to make atonement over it, that it may be sent away into the wilderness to Azazel.
text.Rotherham: but, the goat over which came up the lot for Azazel, shall he cause to stand alive—before Yahweh to put a propitiatory-covering over him,—to send him away unto Azazel, towards the desert.

text.NRSV: Aaron shall present the bull as a sin offering for himself, and shall make atonement for himself and for his house; he shall slaughter the bull as a sin offering for himself.
text.Rotherham: So Aaron shall bring near the sin-bearing bullock which is for himself, and shall put a propitiatory-covering about himself, and about his household,—and shall slay the sin-bearing bullock which is for himself;

text.NRSV: He shall take a censer full of coals of fire from the altar before the Lord, and two handfuls of crushed sweet incense, and he shall bring it inside the curtain
text.Rotherham: and shall take the censer-full of burning coals of fire from off the altar from before Yahweh, and his hands full of fragrant incense, beaten small,—and bring within the veil;

text.NRSV: and put the incense on the fire before the Lord, that the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat that is upon the covenant, or he will die.
text.Rotherham: and shall put the incense upon the fire before Yahweh,—and the cloud of incense shall cover the propitiatory which is over the testimony, and he shall not die.

text.NRSV: He shall take some of the blood of the bull, and sprinkle it with his finger on the front of the mercy seat, and before the mercy seat he shall sprinkle the blood seven times.
text.Rotherham: Then shall he take of the blood of the bullock, and shall sprinkle with his finger upon the face of the propitiatory, eastwards,—and before the propitiatory, shall he sprinkle seven times, of the blood, with his finger.

text.NRSV: He shall slaughter the goat of the sin offering that is for the people and bring its blood inside the curtain, and do with its blood as he did with the blood of the bull, sprinkling it upon the mercy seat and before the mercy seat.
text.Rotherham: Then shall he slay the sin-bearing goat which is for the people, and bring in its blood, within the veil,—and do with its blood, as he did to the blood of the bullock, and shall sprinkle it, upon the propitiatory and before the propitiatory:

text.NRSV: Thus he shall make atonement for the sanctuary, because of the uncleannesses of the people of Israel, and because of their transgressions, all their sins; and so he shall do for the tent of meeting, which remains with them in the midst of their uncleannesses.
text.Rotherham: so shall he put a propitiatory-covering, over the holy place because of the uncleanness of the sons of Israel, and because of their transgressions to the extent of all their sins,—and, so, shall he do for the tent of meeting which abideth with them, in the midst of their uncleanness.

text.NRSV: No one shall be in the tent of meeting from the time he enters to make atonement in the sanctuary until he comes out and has made atonement for himself and for his house and for all the assembly of Israel.
text.Rotherham: And no human being, shall be in the tent of meeting, when he cometh in to make a covering by propitiation in the holy place, until he goeth out,—so shall he put a propitiatory-covering about himself and about his household and about all the convocation of Israel.

text.NRSV: Then he shall go out to the altar that is before the Lord and make atonement on its behalf, and shall take some of the blood of the bull and of the blood of the goat, and put it on each of the horns of the altar.
text.Rotherham: Then shall he go out unto the altar which is before Yahweh and shall put a propitiatory-covering thereupon,—and shall take of the blood of the bullock, and of the blood of the goat, and put upon the horns of the altar, round about;

text.NRSV: He shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times, and cleanse it and hallow it from the uncleannesses of the people of Israel.
text.Rotherham: and shall sprinkle upon it, of the blood with his finger seven times,—and shall cleanse it and hallow it, from the uncleannesses of the sons of Israel.

text.NRSV: When he has finished atoning for the holy place and the tent of meeting and the altar, he shall present the live goat.
text.Rotherham: And, when he hath made an end of covering by propitiation the holy place, and the tent of meeting, and the altar, then shall he bring near the living goat.

text.NRSV: Then Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat, and confess over it all the iniquities of the people of Israel, and all their transgressions, all their sins, putting them on the head of the goat, and sending it away into the wilderness by means of someone designated for the task.
text.Rotherham: And Aaron shall lean his two hands upon the head of the living goat, and confess over him, all the iniquities of the sons of Israel, and all their transgressions, to the extent of all their sins,—and shall put them upon the head of the goat, and then send him away, by the hand of a man appointed towards the desert:

text.NRSV: The goat shall bear on itself all their iniquities to a barren region; and the goat shall be set free in the wilderness.
text.Rotherham: so shall the goat bear upon him all their iniquities into a lone land,—and he shall set the goat free, in the desert.

text.NRSV: Then Aaron shall enter the tent of meeting, and shall take off the linen vestments that he put on when he went into the holy place, and shall leave them there.
text.Rotherham: Then shall Aaron come into the tent of meeting, and put off the linen garments which he put on when he came into the holy place,—and shall leave them there;

text.NRSV: He shall bathe his body in water in a holy place, and put on his vestments; then he shall come out and offer his burnt offering and the burnt offering of the people, making atonement for himself and for the people.
text.Rotherham: and shall bathe his flesh in water in a holy place, and put on his [other] garments,—and go forth, and offer his own ascending-sacrifice, and the ascending-sacrifice of the people, so shall he put a propitiatory-covering about himself, and about the people;

text.NRSV: The fat of the sin offering he shall turn into smoke on the altar.
text.Rotherham: and, with the fat of the sin-bearer, shall he make a perfume at the altar.

text.NRSV: The one who sets the goat free for Azazel shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and afterward may come into the camp.
text.Rotherham: And he that sent away the goat unto Azazel, shall wash—his clothes, and bathe his flesh in water,—and, after that, shall he come into the camp.

text.NRSV: The bull of the sin offering and the goat of the sin offering, whose blood was brought in to make atonement in the holy place, shall be taken outside the camp; their skin and their flesh and their dung shall be consumed in fire.
